ground_truth.title: How to File Your Own Taxes: 6 Steps for Beginners
ground_truth.main_content:
Filing your federal income tax return can seem overwhelming. But you can tackle tax season one step at a time—and avoid rookie mistakes—while you take advantage of money-saving opportunities.

Step 1: Keep an eye on your income. You need to file a tax return if you meet or surpass certain levels of income during the year. If you're employed, look at your pay stub for the "year to date" income—and if you have more than one job, be sure to add up your income from all your employers and any gig work. Remember to include income from other sources, too, such as money you make on rental property, anything you sell, investments or interest.

Step 2: Save the right paperwork all year long. Stay on top of tax-related paperwork throughout the year; it will make your life easier during tax season. You might want to keep receipts for things like charitable donations, work-related expenses and medical bills, or other items. You may also want to keep statements from student loans or investments and any grants or fellowships. Having these handy and organized can help you determine whether to itemize or take the standard deduction, and make the process easier. The IRS recommends keeping records for at least seven years.

Step 3: Watch for your income documents to arrive. You should receive forms about how much income you've earned from your employers and other income sources in January or February. If you are an employee, you will receive a Form W-2 detailing your earnings, as well as which taxes were withheld. If you work freelance or on a contract, you may receive a Form 1099-NEC detailing what you earned. You may also receive documents showing dividends or interest earned on investments (Forms 1099-DIV or 1099-INT, for example), or student loan interest you've paid (Form 1098-E). If you're a college student (or you have a dependent who is), you'll receive a Form 1098-T that shows how much you paid in tuition, as well as any amounts you received from grants or fellowships.

Quick Tip: You shouldn't file your tax return until you've received a Form W-2 or Form 1099 from every place you have worked during the year. When it comes time to file, you will use those documents to fill out a Form 1040—the IRS form for individual income taxes.

Step 4: Learn which credits and deductions you can take. Getting a sense of which credits and deductions you may be eligible for can help you pull together the proper documentation. Consider the Saver's credit, which you may be eligible for if you contribute to a retirement plan. You may also deduct up to $2,500 in student loan interest payments, depending on your modified adjusted gross income. Generally, you may deduct qualified charitable donations if you itemize your federal income tax return. If you are self-employed, you may be able to claim deductions for work-related expenses such as industry subscriptions and office supplies.

Step 5: Mind your deadlines. Having your tax documents arrive in January or February gives you about two months to prepare your tax return by the standard due date of April 15. In general, experts recommend filing tax returns earlier rather than later. The earlier you file, the better your chances of avoiding tax-related identity theft. Plus, if you're owed a tax refund, you may receive it sooner.

Quick Tip: Think you need some extra time to file? Generally, you can file an extension giving you an extra six months to submit your return, usually in October. But if you owe the government money, you still need to pay your tax liability in full by the due date, typically April 15, to avoid penalties and interest.

Step 6: Decide how to file your tax return. There are several options for preparing and filing your tax return: "Free File" if your adjusted gross income is less than a certain limit; IRS online forms if your adjusted gross income is higher; tax preparation software if you want more guidance; or a tax preparer if you need one-on-one help from an expert.

Quick Tip: Remember that you may need to prepare and file state and/or local tax returns in addition to your federal return.
